Final Response to the Assignment

Komla

2025-02-27

Introduction

This document presents the analysis of the dataset contained in the Excel file stats_R_Python_questions_2.xlsx. The assignment consists of three main parts:

  1. Summary Statistics and Plotting:
    Compute the mean and standard deviation of the digital counts and plot these values (Mean ± SD) over time for each marker and treatment group.

  2. T-Test Comparisons (DAY1 vs. DAY8):
    Test whether DAY8 and DAY1 significantly differ at α = 0.05 under each treatment for each marker. A paired t-test is used when the same subjects are measured at both time points; otherwise, an unpaired t-test is applied.

  3. Mixed-Effects Modeling, Residual Analysis, and Post Hoc Comparisons:
    Assess the constant variance assumption via a residuals vs. fitted plot, fit mixed-effects models for each marker, and report both the unadjusted and Bonferroni-adjusted p-values for the DAY22 vs. DAY8 contrast.

Below, each step is described with the corresponding code.

1. Summary Statistics and Plotting

1.a) Compute Mean and Standard Deviation

# Load necessary libraries
library(readxl)
library(tidyverse)
library(ggplot2)
library(lme4)
library(lmerTest)
library(broom)
library(broom.mixed)
library(emmeans)
library(knitr)  # for kable()

# Set the working directory (adjust this )
setwd("/Users/komlagnona/Desktop/MYDESK/MYWORK")

# Define file path (adjust this)
file_path <- "/Users/komlagnona/Desktop/MYDESK/MYWORK/stats_R_Python_questions_2.xlsx"

# Load the "data" sheet
data <- read_excel(file_path, sheet = "data")

# Check data structure and summary statistics
str(data)
## tibble [254 × 5] (S3: tbl_df/tbl/data.frame)
##  $ sbj_id       : chr [1:254] "A" "A" "A" "B" ...
##  $ sample_time  : chr [1:254] "DAY1" "DAY1" "DAY1" "DAY1" ...
##  $ digital_count: num [1:254] 169 207 10 636 821 ...
##  $ marker       : chr [1:254] "C4" "C8" "TG" "C4" ...
##  $ trt_group    : chr [1:254] "TA" "TA" "TA" "TA" ...
summary(data)
##     sbj_id          sample_time        digital_count       marker         
##  Length:254         Length:254         Min.   :   4.0   Length:254        
##  Class :character   Class :character   1st Qu.: 100.5   Class :character  
##  Mode  :character   Mode  :character   Median : 292.0   Mode  :character  
##                                        Mean   : 569.4                     
##                                        3rd Qu.: 572.5                     
##                                        Max.   :6711.0                     
##   trt_group        
##  Length:254        
##  Class :character  
##  Mode  :character  
##                    
##                    
## 
# Compute mean and standard deviation for digital_count by marker, sample_time, and trt_group
summary_stats <- data %>%
  group_by(marker, sample_time, trt_group) %>%
  summarise(
    mean_digital_count = mean(digital_count, na.rm = TRUE),
    sd_digital_count = sd(digital_count, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  as.data.frame()

# View the results in a nicely formatted table
knitr::kable(summary_stats, caption = "Summary Statistics: Mean and SD by Marker, Sample Time, and Treatment Group")

1.b) Plot Mean ± SD Over Time for Each Marker and Treatment

# Convert sample_time to an ordered factor before plotting
summary_stats <- summary_stats %>%
  mutate(sample_time = factor(sample_time, levels = c("DAY1", "DAY8", "DAY15", "DAY22", "DAY29")))

# Create the plot using ggplot2
summary_plots <- ggplot(summary_stats, aes(x = sample_time, y = mean_digital_count, 
                                            color = trt_group, group = trt_group)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +  
  geom_point(size = 2) +  
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = mean_digital_count - sd_digital_count, 
                    ymax = mean_digital_count + sd_digital_count), width = 0.2) +  
  facet_wrap(~ marker, scales = "fixed") +
  labs(title = "Marker Readout Over Time",
       x = "Sample Time", 
       y = "Mean Digital Count",
       color = "Treatment Group") +
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Dark2") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 18, face = "bold", hjust = 0.5, color = "black"), # Centered title
    axis.title.x = element_text(size = 18, color = "black"),  # X-axis label
    axis.title.y = element_text(size = 18, color = "black"),  # Y-axis label
    axis.text.x = element_text(size = 14, face = "bold",color = "black"),   # X-axis tick labels
    axis.text.y = element_text(size = 14, face = "bold",color = "black"),   # Y-axis tick labels
    legend.title = element_text(size = 16, color = "black"),  # Legend title
    legend.text = element_text(size = 16, color = "black"),   # Legend text
    strip.text = element_text(size = 14, face = "bold", color = "black")  # Facet titles
  )

# View the plot
print(summary_plots)

#2. T-Test Comparisons (DAY1 vs. DAY8)

Let’s test whether DAY8 and DAY1 significantly differ under each treatment for each marker. I will use a paired t-test if the same subjects are measured at both time points, and an unpaired t-test otherwise.

# Filter data for DAY1 and DAY8 only
day1_day8_data <- data %>% 
  filter(sample_time %in% c("DAY1", "DAY8"))

# Perform t-tests for each marker and treatment group
t_test_results <- day1_day8_data %>%
  group_by(marker, trt_group) %>%
  summarise(
    test = list({
      df <- pick(everything())
      # Identify unique subject IDs for each day
      subs_day1 <- unique(df$sbj_id[df$sample_time == "DAY1"])
      subs_day8 <- unique(df$sbj_id[df$sample_time == "DAY8"])
      
      # Check if the same subjects are present (after sorting)
      if (length(subs_day1) == length(subs_day8) && all(sort(subs_day1) == sort(subs_day8))) {
        # For paired t-test: sort by subject ID to ensure proper pairing
        x_vals <- df %>% 
          filter(sample_time == "DAY1") %>% 
          arrange(sbj_id) %>% 
          pull(digital_count)
        y_vals <- df %>% 
          filter(sample_time == "DAY8") %>% 
          arrange(sbj_id) %>% 
          pull(digital_count)
        
        t.test(x = x_vals, y = y_vals, paired = TRUE)
      } else {
        # Otherwise, use an unpaired t-test
        x_vals <- df %>% filter(sample_time == "DAY1") %>% pull(digital_count)
        y_vals <- df %>% filter(sample_time == "DAY8") %>% pull(digital_count)
        
        t.test(x = x_vals, y = y_vals, paired = FALSE)
      }
    }),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    results = map(test, tidy)
  ) %>%
  unnest(results) %>%
  select(marker, trt_group, p.value, estimate, statistic)

# View the t-test results in a nicely formatted table
knitr::kable(t_test_results, caption = "T-Test Results (DAY1 vs. DAY8) by Marker and Treatment Group")
T-Test Results (DAY1 vs. DAY8) by Marker and Treatment Group
marker trt_group p.value estimate statistic
C4 TA 0.0312945 -417.14286 -2.7968271
C4 TB 0.4693234 -438.14286 -0.7531563
C4 TC 0.6013570 47.67857 0.5418475
C8 TA 0.1213232 -446.85714 -1.8037349
C8 TB 0.6745258 -690.42857 -0.4337380
C8 TC 0.9250361 -31.71429 -0.0967895
TG TA 0.0729864 -206.42857 -2.1679333
TG TB 0.0682489 -183.37143 -2.1584265
TG TC 0.1507046 -33.07143 -1.8101550 
# Select only significant results (p < 0.05)
significant_t_test_results <- filter(t_test_results, p.value < 0.05)
selected_markers           <- significant_t_test_results$marker
selected_trt_group         <- significant_t_test_results$trt_group

# Print summary statement for significant markers
cat("DAY1 and DAY8 differ for the following marker(s):", 
    selected_markers, "in the treatment group(s):", selected_trt_group, "\n")
## DAY1 and DAY8 differ for the following marker(s): C4 in the treatment group(s): TA

Summary.

  • The difference in treatments between DAY8 and DAY1 is significantly different (P < 0.05) for marker C4 under treatment TA. Note: the evidence is suggestive (p < 0.1) for marker TG under treatments TA and TB

#3. Mixed-Effects Modeling, Residual Analysis, and Post Hoc Comparisons

3.a) Residuals vs. Fitted Plot

First, let’s assess any departure from the assumptions unlying the tukey test. We already knowthe experimental units are randomly drawn from a population. That said, let’s check for the constant variance (homoscedasticity) assumption by plotting the residuals versus the fitted values for each marker.

# Function to fit the model and append residuals and fitted values for a given marker
analyze_marker_resid <- function(df) {
  model <- lmer(digital_count ~ sample_time + trt_group + (1 | sbj_id), data = df)
  df %>% mutate(fitted = fitted(model), residuals = resid(model))
}

# Apply the function to each marker and combine the results
resid_data <- data %>%
  group_by(marker) %>%
  group_modify(~ analyze_marker_resid(.x)) %>%
  ungroup()

# Create the Residuals vs. Fitted plot for all markers
p <- ggplot(resid_data, aes(x = fitted, y = residuals)) +
  geom_point(color = "blue") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, color = "black", linetype = "dashed") +
  facet_wrap(~ marker, scales = "free") +
  labs(title = "Residuals vs Fitted Values by Marker",
       x = "Fitted Values",
       y = "Residuals") +
  theme_minimal()

#
p<- p + theme(text = element_text(size = 20), plot.title = element_text(size = 16, face = "bold", hjust = 0.5, color = "black"))
# Visualize the plot
print(p)

Summary.

  • The constant variance assumption appears to be reasonable for some markers than the others.For instance, I suspect heteroskedasticity for marker C4. That said, I will not use the Turkey test for inference here. I will report the unadjusted p-values along with the corresponding adjusted p-values after multiple testing correction.

3.b) Mixed-Effects Model with Post Hoc Comparisons

Here, i fit a mixed-effects model for each marker, extract the treatment effect p-values, and perform pairwise comparisons for the contrast between DAY22 and DAY8. Both unadjusted and Bonferroni-adjusted p-values are reported.

marker_results <- data %>%
  group_by(marker) %>%
  nest() %>%
  mutate(
    # Fit the model for each marker
    model = map(data, ~ lmer(digital_count ~ sample_time + trt_group + (1 | sbj_id), data = .x)),
    
    # Extract the ANOVA table and get the p-value for the treatment effect
    anova_out = map(model, ~ anova(.x)),
    trt_p_value = map_dbl(anova_out, ~ .x["trt_group", "Pr(>F)"]),
    
    # Compute estimated marginal means for sample_time
    emms = map(model, ~ emmeans(.x, ~ sample_time)),
    
    # Get unadjusted pairwise comparisons among sample_time levels
    pairwise_unadj = map(emms, ~ contrast(.x, method = "pairwise", adjust = "none")),
    
    # Get Bonferroni-adjusted pairwise comparisons among sample_time levels
    pairwise_bonf = map(emms, ~ contrast(.x, method = "pairwise", adjust = "bonferroni")),
    
    # Extract the unadjusted p-value for the contrast between DAY22 and DAY8
    day22_day8_p_unadj = map_dbl(pairwise_unadj, ~ {
      tmp <- as.data.frame(.x) %>% filter(contrast %in% c("DAY22 - DAY8", "DAY8 - DAY22"))
      if(nrow(tmp) > 0) tmp$p.value[1] else NA_real_
    }),
    
    # Extract the Bonferroni-adjusted p-value for the contrast between DAY22 and DAY8
    day22_day8_p_bonf = map_dbl(pairwise_bonf, ~ {
      tmp <- as.data.frame(.x) %>% filter(contrast %in% c("DAY22 - DAY8", "DAY8 - DAY22")) #making sure the proper test is extracted. DAY 22-DAY8 is equivalent of DAY8 - DAY22                                         
      if(nrow(tmp) > 0) tmp$p.value[1] else NA_real_
    })
  ) %>%
  select(marker, trt_p_value, day22_day8_p_unadj, day22_day8_p_bonf)

# View the mixed model results in a nicely formatted table
knitr::kable(marker_results, caption = "Mixed-Effects Model Results with Unadjusted and Bonferroni-adjusted p-values")
Mixed-Effects Model Results with Unadjusted and Bonferroni-adjusted p-values
marker trt_p_value day22_day8_p_unadj day22_day8_p_bonf
C4 0.2406997 0.0055407 0.0554073
C8 0.1502659 0.0075996 0.0759965
TG 0.7247242 0.0768486 0.7684857

Summary.

Here, I report the unadjusted and adjusted p-values. I opted to use Bonferroni correction to adjust for multiple testing and to control for the family wise error rate.

  • The p-values for the treatment effect of all markers are > 0.05. In other words, there is not enough statistical evidence to conclude that the treatment (trt_group) has an effect on the marker digital_count at the 5% significance level

  • Aslo, we do not have enough evidence to conclude that the difference in digital_count between DAY22 and DAY8 for all markers is significant (p > 0.05).

  • Note: the evidence is suggestive (p-val < 0.1) for markers C4 & C8
